question:
An integer $n$, unknown to you, has been randomly chosen in the
interval $[1, 2002]$ with uniform probability. Your objective is
to select $n$ in an \textbf{odd} number of guesses. After
each incorrect guess, you are informed whether $n$ is higher
or lower, and you \textbf{must} guess an integer on your next turn
among the numbers that are still feasibly correct. Show that you
have a strategy so that the chance of winning is greater than $2/3$.
solution:
Use the following strategy: guess $1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, \dots$
until the target number $n$ is revealed to be equal to or lower than one
of these guesses. If $n \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an
odd turn. If $n \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an even turn.
If $n \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, then $n+1$ will be guessed on an even turn,
forcing a guess of $n$ on the next turn. Thus the probability
of success with this strategy is $1335/2002 > 2/3$.

Note: for any positive integer $m$, this strategy wins when the
number is being guessed from $[1,m]$ with probability
$\frac{1}{m} \lfloor \frac{2m+1}{3} \rfloor$. We can prove that
this is best possible as follows.
Let $a_m$ denote $m$ times
the probability of winning when playing optimally.  Also, let $b_m$
denote $m$ times the corresponding probability of winning if the
objective is to select the number in an even number of guesses
instead.  (For definiteness, extend the definitions to incorporate
$a_0 = 0$ and $b_0=0$.)

We first claim that $a_m = 1 + \max_{1\leq k\leq m} \{b_{k-1} +
b_{m-k}\}$ and $b_m = \max_{1\leq k\leq m} \{a_{k-1} + a_{m-k}\}$ for $m
\geq 1$.  To establish the first recursive identity, suppose that our
first guess is some integer $k$. We automatically win if $n=k$, with
probability $1/m$. If $n<k$, with probability $(k-1)/m$, then we wish
to guess an integer in $[1,k-1]$ in an even number of guesses; the
probability of success when playing optimally is $b_{k-1}/(k-1)$, by
assumption.  Similarly, if $n<k$, with probability $(m-k)/m$, then the
subsequent probability of winning is $b_{m-k}/(m-k)$.  In sum, the
overall probability of winning if $k$ is our first guess is
$(1+b_{k-1}+b_{m-k})/m$. For optimal strategy, we choose $k$ such that
this quantity is maximized. (Note that this argument still holds if
$k=1$ or $k=m$, by our definitions of $a_0$ and $b_0$.)  The first
recursion follows, and the second recursion is established similarly.

We now prove by induction that $a_m = \lfloor (2m+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$b_m = \lfloor 2m/3 \rfloor$ for $m \geq 0$. The inductive step relies
on the inequality $\lfloor x \rfloor + \lfloor y \rfloor \leq \lfloor
x+y \rfloor$, with equality when one of $x,y$ is an integer. Now
suppose that $a_i = \lfloor (2i+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$b_i = \lfloor 2i/3 \rfloor$ for $i < m$. Then
\begin{align*}
1+b_{k-1}+b_{m-k} &= 1+\left\lfloor \frac{2(k-1)}{3} \right\rfloor +
\left\lfloor
\frac{2(m-k)}{3} \right\rfloor \\
&\leq \left\lfloor \frac{2m}{3} \right\rfloor
\end{align*}
and similarly $a_{k-1}+a_{m-k} \leq \lfloor (2m+1)/3 \rfloor$, with
equality in both cases attained, e.g., when $k=1$.
The inductive formula for $a_m$ and $b_m$ follows.

descriptive_long.question: An integer $choseninteger$, unknown to you, has been randomly chosen in the interval $[1, 2002]$ with uniform probability. Your objective is to select $choseninteger$ in an \textbf{odd} number of guesses. After each incorrect guess, you are informed whether $choseninteger$ is higher or lower, and you \textbf{must} guess an integer on your next turn among the numbers that are still feasibly correct. Show that you have a strategy so that the chance of winning is greater than $2/3$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Use the following strategy: guess $1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, \dots$ until the target number $choseninteger$ is revealed to be equal to or lower than one of these guesses. If $choseninteger \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an odd turn. If $choseninteger \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an even turn. If $choseninteger \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, then $choseninteger+1$ will be guessed on an even turn, forcing a guess of $choseninteger$ on the next turn. Thus the probability of success with this strategy is $1335/2002 > 2/3$.

Note: for any positive integer $upperlimit$, this strategy wins when the number is being guessed from $[1,upperlimit]$ with probability $\frac{1}{upperlimit} \left\lfloor \frac{2\,upperlimit+1}{3} \right\rfloor$. We can prove that this is best possible as follows.
Let $oddprobm$ denote $upperlimit$ times the probability of winning when playing optimally.  Also, let $evenprobm$ denote $upperlimit$ times the corresponding probability of winning if the objective is to select the number in an even number of guesses instead.  (For definiteness, extend the definitions to incorporate $oddprobzero = 0$ and $evenprobzero=0$.)

We first claim that $oddprobm = 1 + \max_{1\leq firstguess\leq upperlimit} \{\,evenprobkminusone + evenprobmminusk\}$ and $evenprobm = \max_{1\leq firstguess\leq upperlimit} \{\,oddprobkminusone + oddprobmminusk\}$ for $upperlimit \geq 1$.  To establish the first recursive identity, suppose that our first guess is some integer $firstguess$. We automatically win if $choseninteger=firstguess$, with probability $1/upperlimit$. If $choseninteger<firstguess$, with probability $(firstguess-1)/upperlimit$, then we wish to guess an integer in $[1,firstguess-1]$ in an even number of guesses; the probability of success when playing optimally is $evenprobkminusone/(firstguess-1)$, by assumption.  Similarly, if $choseninteger>firstguess$, with probability $(upperlimit-firstguess)/upperlimit$, then the subsequent probability of winning is $evenprobmminusk/(upperlimit-firstguess)$.  In sum, the overall probability of winning if $firstguess$ is our first guess is $(1+evenprobkminusone+evenprobmminusk)/upperlimit$. For an optimal strategy, we choose $firstguess$ such that this quantity is maximized. (Note that this argument still holds if $firstguess=1$ or $firstguess=upperlimit$, by our definitions of $oddprobzero$ and $evenprobzero$.)  The first recursion follows, and the second recursion is established similarly.

We now prove by induction that $oddprobm = \left\lfloor (2\,upperlimit+1)/3 \right\rfloor$ and $evenprobm = \left\lfloor 2\,upperlimit/3 \right\rfloor$ for $upperlimit \geq 0$. The inductive step relies on the inequality $\lfloor realvalue \rfloor + \lfloor anothervar \rfloor \leq \lfloor realvalue+anothervar \rfloor$, with equality when one of $realvalue,anothervar$ is an integer. Now suppose that $oddprobind = \left\lfloor (2\,indexvar+1)/3 \right\rfloor$ and $evenprobind = \left\lfloor 2\,indexvar/3 \right\rfloor$ for $indexvar < upperlimit$. Then
\begin{align*}
1+evenprobkminusone+evenprobmminusk &= 1+\left\lfloor \frac{2\,(firstguess-1)}{3} \right\rfloor + \left\lfloor \frac{2\,(upperlimit-firstguess)}{3} \right\rfloor \\ &\leq \left\lfloor \frac{2\,upperlimit}{3} \right\rfloor
\end{align*}
and similarly $oddprobkminusone+oddprobmminusk \leq \left\lfloor (2\,upperlimit+1)/3 \right\rfloor$, with equality in both cases attained, e.g., when $firstguess=1$.
The inductive formula for $oddprobm$ and $evenprobm$ follows.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
An integer $blueberry$, unknown to you, has been randomly chosen in the
interval $[1, 2002]$ with uniform probability. Your objective is
to select $blueberry$ in an \textbf{odd} number of guesses. After
each incorrect guess, you are informed whether $blueberry$ is higher
or lower, and you \textbf{must} guess an integer on your next turn
among the numbers that are still feasibly correct. Show that you
have a strategy so that the chance of winning is greater than $2/3$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Use the following strategy: guess $1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, \dots$
until the target number $blueberry$ is revealed to be equal to or lower than one
of these guesses. If $blueberry \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an
odd turn. If $blueberry \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an even turn.
If $blueberry \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, then $blueberry+1$ will be guessed on an even turn,
forcing a guess of $blueberry$ on the next turn. Thus the probability
of success with this strategy is $1335/2002 > 2/3$.

Note: for any positive integer $raspberry$, this strategy wins when the
number is being guessed from $[1,raspberry]$ with probability
$\frac{1}{raspberry} \lfloor \frac{2raspberry+1}{3} \rfloor$. We can prove that
this is best possible as follows.
Let $lollipop$ denote $raspberry$ times
the probability of winning when playing optimally.  Also, let $marshmallow$
denote $raspberry$ times the corresponding probability of winning if the
objective is to select the number in an even number of guesses
instead.  (For definiteness, extend the definitions to incorporate
$basketball = 0$ and $cheeseburger=0$.)

We first claim that $lollipop = 1 + \max_{1\leq crocodile\leq raspberry} \{sunflower +
hummingbird\}$ and $marshmallow = \max_{1\leq crocodile\leq raspberry} \{watermelon + screwdriver\}$ for $raspberry
\geq 1$.  To establish the first recursive identity, suppose that our
first guess is some integer $crocodile$. We automatically win if $blueberry=crocodile$, with
probability $1/raspberry$. If $blueberry<crocodile$, with probability $(crocodile-1)/raspberry$, then we wish
to guess an integer in $[1,crocodile-1]$ in an even number of guesses; the
probability of success when playing optimally is $sunflower/(crocodile-1)$, by
assumption.  Similarly, if $blueberry<crocodile$, with probability $(raspberry-crocodile)/raspberry$, then the
subsequent probability of winning is $hummingbird/(raspberry-crocodile)$.  In sum, the
overall probability of winning if $crocodile$ is our first guess is
$(1+sunflower+hummingbird)/raspberry$. For optimal strategy, we choose $crocodile$ such that
this quantity is maximized. (Note that this argument still holds if
$crocodile=1$ or $crocodile=raspberry$, by our definitions of $basketball$ and $cheeseburger$.)  The first
recursion follows, and the second recursion is established similarly.

We now prove by induction that $lollipop = \lfloor (2raspberry+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$marshmallow = \lfloor 2raspberry/3 \rfloor$ for $raspberry \geq 0$. The inductive step relies
on the inequality $\lfloor toothpaste \rfloor + \lfloor skateboard \rfloor \leq \lfloor
toothpaste+skateboard \rfloor$, with equality when one of $toothpaste,skateboard$ is an integer. Now
suppose that $flashlight = \lfloor (2woodpecker+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$microphone = \lfloor 2woodpecker/3 \rfloor$ for $woodpecker < raspberry$. Then
\begin{align*}
1+sunflower+hummingbird &= 1+\left\lfloor \frac{2(crocodile-1)}{3} \right\rfloor +
\left\lfloor
\frac{2(raspberry-crocodile)}{3} \right\rfloor \\
&\leq \left\lfloor \frac{2raspberry}{3} \right\rfloor
\end{align*}
and similarly $watermelon+screwdriver \leq \lfloor (2raspberry+1)/3 \rfloor$, with
equality in both cases attained, e.g., when $crocodile=1$.
The inductive formula for $lollipop$ and $marshmallow$ follows.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
An integer $deterministicconstant$, unknown to you, has been randomly chosen in the
interval $[1, 2002]$ with uniform probability. Your objective is
to select $deterministicconstant$ in an \textbf{odd} number of guesses. After
each incorrect guess, you are informed whether $deterministicconstant$ is higher
or lower, and you \textbf{must} guess an integer on your next turn
among the numbers that are still feasibly correct. Show that you
have a strategy so that the chance of winning is greater than $2/3$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Use the following strategy: guess $1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, \dots$
until the target number $deterministicconstant$ is revealed to be equal to or lower than one
of these guesses. If $deterministicconstant \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an
odd turn. If $deterministicconstant \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an even turn.
If $deterministicconstant \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, then $deterministicconstant+1$ will be guessed on an even turn,
forcing a guess of $deterministicconstant$ on the next turn. Thus the probability
of success with this strategy is $1335/2002 > 2/3$.

Note: for any positive integer $lowerbound$, this strategy wins when the
number is being guessed from $[1,lowerbound]$ with probability
$\frac{1}{lowerbound} \lfloor \frac{2lowerbound+1}{3} \rfloor$. We can prove that
this is best possible as follows.
Let $failuremeasure$ denote $lowerbound$ times
the probability of winning when playing optimally.  Also, let $suffermeasure$
denote $lowerbound$ times the corresponding probability of winning if the
objective is to select the number in an even number of guesses
instead.  (For definiteness, extend the definitions to incorporate
$failurezero = 0$ and $sufferzero=0$.)

We first claim that $failuremeasure = 1 + \max_{1\leq finalanswer\leq lowerbound} \{sufferprecede +
sufferremaining\}$ and $suffermeasure = \max_{1\leq finalanswer\leq lowerbound} \{failureprecede + failureremaining\}$ for $lowerbound
\geq 1$.  To establish the first recursive identity, suppose that our
first guess is some integer $finalanswer$. We automatically win if $deterministicconstant=finalanswer$, with
probability $1/lowerbound$. If $deterministicconstant<finalanswer$, with probability $(finalanswer-1)/lowerbound$, then we wish
to guess an integer in $[1,finalanswer-1]$ in an even number of guesses; the
probability of success when playing optimally is $sufferprecede/(finalanswer-1)$, by
assumption.  Similarly, if $deterministicconstant<finalanswer$, with probability $(lowerbound-finalanswer)/lowerbound$, then the
subsequent probability of winning is $sufferremaining/(lowerbound-finalanswer)$.  In sum, the
overall probability of winning if $finalanswer$ is our first guess is
$(1+sufferprecede+sufferremaining)/lowerbound$. For optimal strategy, we choose $finalanswer$ such that
this quantity is maximized. (Note that this argument still holds if
$finalanswer=1$ or $finalanswer=lowerbound$, by our definitions of $failurezero$ and $sufferzero$.)  The first
recursion follows, and the second recursion is established similarly.

We now prove by induction that $failuremeasure = \lfloor (2lowerbound+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$suffermeasure = \lfloor 2lowerbound/3 \rfloor$ for $lowerbound \geq 0$. The inductive step relies
on the inequality $\lfloor definitevalue \rfloor + \lfloor certainvalue \rfloor \leq \lfloor
definitevalue+certainvalue \rfloor$, with equality when one of $definitevalue,certainvalue$ is an integer. Now
suppose that $failureindex = \lfloor (2wholequantity+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$sufferindex = \lfloor 2wholequantity/3 \rfloor$ for $wholequantity < lowerbound$. Then
\begin{align*}
1+sufferprecede+sufferremaining &= 1+\left\lfloor \frac{2(finalanswer-1)}{3} \right\rfloor +
\left\lfloor
\frac{2(lowerbound-finalanswer)}{3} \right\rfloor \\
&\leq \left\lfloor \frac{2lowerbound}{3} \right\rfloor
\end{align*}
and similarly $failureprecede+failureremaining \leq \lfloor (2lowerbound+1)/3 \rfloor$, with
equality in both cases attained, e.g., when $finalanswer=1$.
The inductive formula for $failuremeasure$ and $suffermeasure$ follows.

garbled_string.question:
An integer $rquxmdve$, unknown to you, has been randomly chosen in the
interval $[1, 2002]$ with uniform probability. Your objective is
to select $rquxmdve$ in an \textbf{odd} number of guesses. After
each incorrect guess, you are informed whether $rquxmdve$ is higher
or lower, and you \textbf{must} guess an integer on your next turn
among the numbers that are still feasibly correct. Show that you
have a strategy so that the chance of winning is greater than $2/3$.
garbled_string.solution:
Use the following strategy: guess $1, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, \dots$
until the target number $rquxmdve$ is revealed to be equal to or lower than one
of these guesses. If $rquxmdve \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an
odd turn. If $rquxmdve \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$, it will be guessed on an even turn.
If $rquxmdve \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$, then $rquxmdve+1$ will be guessed on an even turn,
forcing a guess of $rquxmdve$ on the next turn. Thus the probability
of success with this strategy is $1335/2002 > 2/3$.

Note: for any positive integer $ijztscqo$, this strategy wins when the
number is being guessed from $[1,ijztscqo]$ with probability
$\frac{1}{ijztscqo} \lfloor \frac{2ijztscqo+1}{3} \rfloor$. We can prove that
this is best possible as follows.
Let $rpjvsket$ denote $ijztscqo$ times
the probability of winning when playing optimally.  Also, let $udnhpoix$
denote $ijztscqo$ times the corresponding probability of winning if the
objective is to select the number in an even number of guesses
instead.  (For definiteness, extend the definitions to incorporate
$cvgzawlr = 0$ and $lpnxtrqe=0$.)

We first claim that $rpjvsket = 1 + \max_{1\leq vebhlany\leq ijztscqo} \{kxwqzvda +
wsvilcpe\}$ and $udnhpoix = \max_{1\leq vebhlany\leq ijztscqo} \{fmyoabnj + hqzefkyn\}$ for $ijztscqo
\geq 1$.  To establish the first recursive identity, suppose that our
first guess is some integer $vebhlany$. We automatically win if $rquxmdve=vebhlany$, with
probability $1/ijztscqo$. If $rquxmdve<vebhlany$, with probability $(vebhlany-1)/ijztscqo$, then we wish
to guess an integer in $[1,vebhlany-1]$ in an even number of guesses; the
probability of success when playing optimally is $kxwqzvda/(vebhlany-1)$, by
assumption.  Similarly, if $rquxmdve<vebhlany$, with probability $(ijztscqo-vebhlany)/ijztscqo$, then the
subsequent probability of winning is $wsvilcpe/(ijztscqo-vebhlany)$.  In sum, the
overall probability of winning if $vebhlany$ is our first guess is
$(1+kxwqzvda+wsvilcpe)/ijztscqo$. For optimal strategy, we choose $vebhlany$ such that
this quantity is maximized. (Note that this argument still holds if
$vebhlany=1$ or $vebhlany=ijztscqo$, by our definitions of $cvgzawlr$ and $lpnxtrqe$.)  The first
recursion follows, and the second recursion is established similarly.

We now prove by induction that $rpjvsket = \lfloor (2ijztscqo+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$udnhpoix = \lfloor 2ijztscqo/3 \rfloor$ for $ijztscqo \geq 0$. The inductive step relies
on the inequality $\lfloor qzfuntay \rfloor + \lfloor mlbrovki \rfloor \leq \lfloor
qzfuntay+mlbrovki \rfloor$, with equality when one of $qzfuntay,mlbrovki$ is an integer. Now
suppose that $adplsiwm = \lfloor (2smgefhuc+1)/3 \rfloor$ and
$jrwodgku = \lfloor 2smgefhuc/3 \rfloor$ for $smgefhuc < ijztscqo$. Then
\begin{align*}
1+kxwqzvda+wsvilcpe &= 1+\left\lfloor \frac{2(vebhlany-1)}{3} \right\rfloor +
\left\lfloor
\frac{2(ijztscqo-vebhlany)}{3} \right\rfloor \\
&\leq \left\lfloor \frac{2ijztscqo}{3} \right\rfloor
\end{align*}
and similarly $fmyoabnj+hqzefkyn \leq \lfloor (2ijztscqo+1)/3 \rfloor$, with
equality in both cases attained, e.g., when $vebhlany=1$.
The inductive formula for $rpjvsket$ and $udnhpoix$ follows.

kernel_variant.question:
A positive integer n is chosen uniformly at random from the set {1,2,\ldots ,1500}. After every wrong guess you are told whether n is higher or lower, and on the next move you must guess an integer that is still compatible with all the information you already have.  You want to discover n in an even number of guesses.  

(a) Show that there exists a strategy that succeeds with probability at least 2/3.

(b) Prove that no strategy can succeed with probability larger than 2/3.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout "probability of success" always means the probability - with respect to the uniform choice of the hidden number - that the first correct guess is made on an even move.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A 3-spaced monotone strategy that always ends on an even turn
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Let (g_i)_{i\ge 1} be the sequence
      g_{2r-1}=3r-1,   g_{2r}=3r     (r=1,2,\dots) :
      2,3,5,6,8,9, \ldots 

Keep guessing g_1,g_2,\ldots  until one of them is not smaller than n.
* If some g_i equals n we are done.
* If some g_i exceeds n then n lies between g_{i-1} and g_i.  By the
definition of the sequence this interval contains a single integer, so
the very next guess must be that integer and is therefore correct.
The overshoot can occur only for odd i, hence the winning guess is
always number i+1, which is even.  Consequently the procedure always
terminates on an even turn.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  When does the strategy win?
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Write n=3r, 3r-1 or 3r-2.
* n=3r  (n\equiv 0 mod 3).  Up to step 2r-1 every guess is too small; on step
 2r we guess g_{2r}=3r=n and win on an even turn.
* n=3r-2  (n\equiv 1 mod 3).  Step 2r-1 is g_{2r-1}=3r-1>n, so we overshoot;
 the only remaining admissible value is 3r-2, which we guess on step
 2r and win.
* n=3r-1  (n\equiv 2 mod 3).  Step 2r-1 hits n, but this happens on an odd
 turn, so here the strategy fails.
Hence the algorithm succeeds exactly for the 1000 numbers that are 0 or
1 (mod 3) among the 1500 possible targets, giving success probability
1000/1500 = 2/3.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  An upper bound of 2/3 for every even-turn strategy
---------------------------------------------------------------------
For an interval of length m define
  a_m = maximum number of inputs for which a player can force a win on
        an *odd* move;
  b_m = maximum number of inputs for which a player can force a win on
        an *even* move.
Set a_0=b_0=0.

Suppose the current interval has length m and the first guess is k
(1\leq k\leq m).
* We win immediately if n=k (1 input).
* If n<k (k-1 inputs) we are left with the *even-turn* problem on an
  interval of length k-1, so at most b_{k-1} of those inputs can still
  be winning.
* If n>k (m-k inputs) we again face an even-turn problem, now of length
  m-k, giving at most b_{m-k} further winners.
Thus
     a_m = 1 + max_{1\leq k\leq m}( b_{k-1}+b_{m-k} ).        (1)

If the very first move of an even-turn strategy is k, then after the
answer we always have an *odd-turn* sub-problem, whence
     b_m =     max_{1\leq k\leq m}( a_{k-1}+a_{m-k} ).        (2)

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Claim.  For every m\geq 0
        a_m = \lfloor (2m+1)/3\rfloor   and  b_m = \lfloor 2m/3\rfloor .          (3)
---------------------------------------------------------------------
The proof is by induction on m.

Base cases m=0,1,2 are easily checked directly.

Induction step.  Assume (3) holds for all indices < m.

Upper bounds
------------
Take any k (1\leq k\leq m).  Using the induction hypothesis and \lfloor x\rfloor \leq x,
 1 + b_{k-1}+b_{m-k} \leq  1 + 2(k-1)/3 + 2(m-k)/3 = (2m+1)/3,
so by (1)   a_m \leq  \lfloor (2m+1)/3\rfloor .
Similarly
 a_{k-1}+a_{m-k} \leq  (2(k-1)+1)/3 + (2(m-k)+1)/3 = 2m/3,
so (2) yields   b_m \leq  \lfloor 2m/3\rfloor .

Achieving equality - a case analysis of m (mod 3)
-------------------------------------------------
We now show that the above upper bounds can always be attained; the
choices of k depend only on the residue of m modulo 3.

Write m=3q,r with r\in {0,1,2}.

*  r=0  (m=3q).
   - Equation (1).  Choose k=3.
     Then k-1=2 and m-k = 3q-3=3(q-1).
     Using the induction hypothesis,
       1+b_{k-1}+b_{m-k}=1+b_{2}+b_{3(q-1)}
       =1+1+2(q-1)=2q = \lfloor (2m+1)/3\rfloor .
   - Equation (2).  Choose k=m-1=3q-1 (which is \equiv 2 mod 3).
     Then k-1=3q-2 and m-k=1.  Hence
       a_{k-1}+a_{m-k}=a_{3q-2}+a_1
       =(2q-1)+1 = 2q = \lfloor 2m/3\rfloor .

*  r=1  (m=3q+1).
   - For (1) take k=1.  Then m-k=3q and
       1+b_{k-1}+b_{m-k}=1+b_0+b_{3q}=1+0+2q=2q+1
       = \lfloor (2m+1)/3\rfloor .
   - For (2) choose k=3.  Now k-1=2 and m-k=3q-2; hence
       a_{k-1}+a_{m-k}=a_2+a_{3q-2}=1+(2q-1)=2q
       = \lfloor 2m/3\rfloor .

*  r=2  (m=3q+2).
   - For (1) choose k=3.  We obtain k-1=2,
     m-k = 3q-1 and
       1+b_{k-1}+b_{m-k}=1+b_2+b_{3q-1}=1+1+(2q-1)=2q+1
       = \lfloor (2m+1)/3\rfloor .
   - For (2) pick k=m.  Then k-1=3q+1, m-k=0, so
       a_{k-1}+a_{m-k}=a_{3q+1}+a_0=(2q+1)+0=2q+1=
       \lfloor 2m/3\rfloor .

In every residue class there exists a k that attains the upper bound,
forcing equality in (3).  The induction is complete.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
Consequences for m = 1500
---------------------------------------------------------------------
For m=1500 we have b_{1500}=\lfloor 2\cdot 1500/3\rfloor =1000, i.e. *no* strategy that is
required to finish on an even turn can succeed for more than 1000 of
the 1500 possible hidden numbers.  Hence the maximal success
probability is at most 1000/1500 = 2/3.

---------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Final result
---------------------------------------------------------------------
The 3-spaced guessing strategy of Section 1 wins exactly for the 1000
numbers that are 0 or 1 (mod 3) and always does so on an even turn,
thereby reaching success probability 2/3.  Section 3 shows that no
strategy can do better.  Therefore 2/3 is the optimal success
probability, completing the proof.

core_steps: Use a monotone guessing pattern that repeats every 3 numbers: 1,3,4,6,7,9,… (so guesses land on residues 1,0,1 mod 3 in turns 1,2,3, …).
Show residue-wise:  n≡1 (mod 3) is hit on an odd turn; n≡0 (mod 3) on an even turn; n≡2 (mod 3) is preceded by n+1 on an even turn and then guessed on the next (odd) turn.
Hence two of every three possible n are won on an odd turn; total wins = ⌊(2m+1)/3⌋ for an interval of size m.
(Optional optimality)  Set up recursions for a_m (odd goal) and b_m (even goal), solve by induction to prove the bound is best possible.

slot1.description: Upper end of the interval; any positive integer m (preferably m≢0 (mod 3) if one wants the strict “> 2⁄3” conclusion).

slot2.description: Parity requirement for the successful guess (odd ↔ even); the same recursive/inductive framework works with roles of a_m and b_m swapped.
